method_atom: Analysis of strongly-correlated many-body systems in a regime beyond weak-coupling/mean-field approximations, where interaction-driven correlations dominate. Central machinery: a quantum phase transition governed by the competition between kinetic (hopping/tunneling) energy and on-site interaction strength as a control parameter is tuned; a continuous crossover between a paired/bound-state regime and a collective-condensate regime as the coupling is swept through a resonance; the qualitative change of collective behavior under reduced effective dimensionality; and the macroscopic degeneracy of a lowest quantized energy level induced by an effective transverse field.
object_atom: Dilute, ultracold atomic gases — bosonic and fermionic atoms confined in optical lattices, tuned near Feshbach resonances, restricted to one or two dimensions, or set into fast rotation, exhibiting phenomena such as the Mott-Hubbard transition, the BCS-BEC crossover, and lowest-Landau-level physics.
brief_reason: The kinetic-vs-interaction quantum phase transition, resonance-tuned pairing-to-condensate crossover, and lowest-level degeneracy under an effective field are transferable machinery, while the ultracold bosonic/fermionic atoms in optical lattices, near Feshbach resonances, and under rotation are the atomic-physics-specific object.
